title.rendered: Outlet & Switch Installation — Northern Virginia

content.rendered:
Outlet & Switch Installation

New Outlets, GFCI Upgrades, Smart Switches

Adding outlets where you need them, upgrading two-prong to grounded three-prong, installing GFCI protection in kitchens and bathrooms, and wiring smart switches and dimmers. Licensed, insured, to code.

Call 571-500-6637 Request a Quote

What outlet and switch installation covers

Outlet and switch work is the most common residential electrical service call. It covers adding new outlets in rooms that don’t have enough (home offices, kitchen islands, garage workshops), replacing old two-prong ungrounded outlets with modern grounded three-prong receptacles, installing GFCI outlets in wet locations (kitchens, bathrooms, laundry rooms, garages, exteriors), upgrading to USB-equipped outlets, and wiring smart switches, dimmers, and three-way switch circuits.

The wiring behind the outlet matters more than the outlet itself. Ahmad checks the circuit capacity before adding outlets (you can’t just daisy-chain indefinitely), confirms the grounding path when upgrading from two-prong to three-prong (a ground wire has to actually go somewhere), and installs GFCI protection at the correct points in the circuit so downstream outlets are also protected.

For older homes with ungrounded wiring, the options are GFCI protection (which provides shock protection without a ground wire) or running a new grounded circuit from the panel. Ahmad explains both options and the trade-offs — GFCI is cheaper but doesn’t provide surge protection; a new circuit costs more but gives you a proper ground. You decide, he installs.

title.rendered: Smoke Alarm Installation & Replacement — Northern Virginia

content.rendered:
Smoke Alarm Installation

Hardwired Protection, Every Room

Hardwired smoke and CO detectors with battery backup — installed to current Virginia code, interconnected so every alarm sounds when one triggers. Ahmad handles the wiring, mounting, and testing.

Call 571-500-6637 Request a Quote

What smoke alarm installation covers

Hardwired smoke alarms are connected to your home’s electrical system with a battery backup. When one detector triggers, every interconnected alarm in the house sounds — you hear the alert no matter which floor you’re on. Virginia code requires hardwired, interconnected smoke alarms in specific locations: every bedroom, outside each sleeping area, and on every level of the home including the basement.

Ahmad installs new hardwired smoke and carbon monoxide detectors, replaces aging units (the NFPA recommends replacement every 10 years), adds detectors to rooms that were never wired for them, and brings older homes up to current interconnection requirements. If your home has battery-only detectors and you want to upgrade to hardwired, that’s a wiring job — running new cable from the panel to each detector location and interconnecting them all on the same circuit.

The work is straightforward but the wiring matters. A smoke alarm that isn’t properly interconnected gives you a false sense of protection — it’ll sound in the room where the fire starts, but nowhere else. Ahmad tests every detector after installation: individual alarm, interconnection trigger, and battery backup.

title.rendered: Light Fixture Installation & Chandelier Mounting — Northern Virginia

content.rendered:
Light Fixture Installation

Fixtures Mounted Right, Wired Safe

Chandeliers, recessed cans, under-cabinet LEDs, outdoor security lights — Ahmad mounts, wires, and tests every fixture. No drywall patches from guesswork. Licensed and insured.

Call 571-500-6637 Request a Quote

What light fixture installation covers

Light fixture installation covers everything from swapping a basic ceiling mount to hanging a heavy chandelier on a reinforced junction box. Ahmad handles the electrical side — confirming the box is rated for the fixture weight, verifying the circuit can carry the load, wiring the connections, and testing the dimmer compatibility if you’re using one.

Common calls include chandelier mounting (especially on vaulted or two-story ceilings where the weight rating matters), recessed can installation in finished ceilings, under-cabinet LED strip wiring in kitchen renovations, outdoor security and landscape lighting, and bathroom vanity light replacements where GFCI protection needs to be confirmed.

The difference between a licensed electrician and a handyman on fixture work is the junction box. A fixture that’s too heavy for its box, or a box that’s not properly secured to a joist, is a fire and fall risk that won’t show up until the fixture comes down. Ahmad checks the box before mounting — and replaces it if it’s not rated.

title.rendered: EV Charger Installation — Northern Virginia, DC & Maryland

content.rendered:
EV Charger Installation

Charge at Home by Tomorrow Night

Level 2 EV charger installation — Tesla Wall Connector, ChargePoint, JuiceBox, Wallbox, and every UL-listed unit. Ahmad handles the permit, the circuit, and the install. Written estimate before work starts.

Call 571-500-6637 Request a Quote

What EV charger installation covers

A Level 2 home charger runs on a 240-volt dedicated circuit — the same voltage as your dryer or oven, but on its own breaker. It charges a typical EV battery from near-empty to full overnight (8–10 hours), compared to 24+ hours on a standard 120V outlet. That’s the difference between waking up to a full charge and hoping you have enough range to get through tomorrow.

The installation starts with an on-site panel assessment. Ahmad checks your panel’s remaining capacity, measures the run distance from the panel to your charging location (garage wall, carport, driveway-adjacent exterior), and determines whether your panel can support the new circuit or needs an upgrade first. You get a written estimate before any work is scheduled.

Ahmad installs across every major brand: Tesla Wall Connector, ChargePoint Home Flex, JuiceBox, Wallbox Pulsar Plus, Grizzl-E, Emporia, and OEM units from Ford, Rivian, Hyundai, Kia, and BMW. Hardwired or plug-in — both options are available depending on your charger model and preference. The permit and inspection are included.

title.rendered: Electrical Panel Upgrades — Northern Virginia, DC & Maryland

content.rendered:
Electrical Panel Upgrades

More Capacity, Safer Wiring, One Visit

If your panel can’t keep up with modern loads — EV chargers, heat pumps, kitchen renovations — Ahmad sizes the upgrade, pulls the permit, and does the work. Written estimate before anything starts.

Call 571-500-6637 Request a Quote

What a panel upgrade involves

A panel upgrade replaces your home’s main electrical panel — the breaker box — with a higher-capacity unit. The most common upgrade is from 100 amps to 200 amps, which is now the minimum standard for modern homes. Some homes with heavy loads (EV chargers, heat pumps, hot tubs, workshop circuits) need 300- or 400-amp service.

The job typically takes one full day. Ahmad coordinates with the utility company for a temporary disconnect, installs the new panel with correctly sized breakers for every circuit, re-lands all existing wiring, and brings the grounding and bonding up to current code. The county inspector visits afterward — we meet them, walk through the work, and get the sign-off.

Panel upgrades aren’t optional when they’re needed. A panel that’s tripping under normal load, a panel with Federal Pacific or Zinsco breakers (known fire risks), or a panel that physically can’t accept a new 40-amp or 50-amp breaker for an EV charger — those are the calls that lead here. Ahmad does the on-site assessment, explains what’s needed and what’s not, and gives you a written estimate with no add-on fees on the work day.

title.rendered: Electrical Troubleshooting — Northern Virginia, DC & Maryland

content.rendered:
Electrical Troubleshooting

Fast, Accurate Diagnosis Across Northern Virginia

Flickering lights, tripping breakers, dead outlets — Ahmad Shaban is a Virginia-licensed Master Electrician who finds the root cause and fixes it the first time. Same-day availability in most of our service area.

Call 571-500-6637 Request a Quote

What electrical troubleshooting covers

Electrical troubleshooting is diagnostic work. Before anything gets replaced, swapped, or upgraded, someone has to figure out why the circuit isn’t behaving. That’s what Ahmad does — he reads the panel, runs continuity and voltage tests, isolates the problem to a specific circuit or device, and explains what he found before proposing a fix.

Common calls include intermittent breaker trips, flickering lights that aren’t caused by the bulb, outlets that test dead on a multimeter, GFCI outlets that won’t reset, circuits that show voltage but won’t carry load, and aluminum-wiring hot spots. The diagnostic visit itself is the service — if the fix is straightforward (a bad breaker, a loose neutral, a miswired junction box), Ahmad handles it on the spot. If it’s bigger (a panel replacement, a full circuit re-run), you get a written estimate before any additional work starts.

EV Electric Services holds a Master Electrician license issued by the Commonwealth of Virginia and is fully insured. Ahmad has been doing residential electrical work across Fairfax County and the surrounding region since before opening EV Electric, and troubleshooting is the service line that started the business.

title.rendered: Electrical Panel Upgrades in Tysons, VA

content.rendered:
Home › Panel Upgrades › This page

Electrical Panel Upgrades in Tysons, VA

Modern Panel. Full Capacity. Done Right.

Ahmad Shaban is a Virginia-licensed Master Electrician who upgrades electrical panels across Tysons every week. Whether your panel is out of room, out of date, or out of code — we size it right, permit it, and get you back to full power in one day.

Call 571-500-6637 Request a Quote

What “panel upgrade” actually means

A panel upgrade replaces your home’s electrical panel — the metal box where your circuit breakers live — with a modern panel that can handle the electrical load you actually put on it. If your panel is 60 or 100 amps, your breakers trip regularly, or you’re planning to add an EV charger, hot tub, or major addition, the panel is usually the bottleneck. The upgrade gives your home the capacity and safety features that modern code requires, and it’s the foundation every other electrical project depends on.

The panels we replace most often in Tysons are Federal Pacific Stab-Lok and Zinsco — both documented fire risks with breakers that fail to trip on overcurrent. We also replace fuse boxes, outdated 60-amp and 100-amp panels, and panels that are simply full with no room for new circuits. A standard upgrade goes from whatever you have now to a 200-amp panel with modern breakers, AFCI and GFCI protection where code requires it, and clearly labeled circuits. We handle the Fairfax County permit, the Dominion Energy coordination, and the post-install inspection.

Ahmad Shaban is a Virginia-licensed Master Electrician and the owner of EV Electric Services, headquartered at 9488 Fairfax Blvd in Fairfax — about 10 minutes from Tysons. Ahmad does every panel upgrade personally: the diagnostic visit, the load calculation, the panel swap, and the inspection walkthrough. No subcontractors, no handoffs. The team is small by design — Ahmad and a trained apprentice on every job.

Quick Reference

Common signs you need a Tysons panel upgrade

Six symptoms we see every week in Tysons. Tap any to learn what it might mean.

Breakers trip when I run the microwave and AC together

Your panel can’t sustain the combined load on that circuit — or across the whole panel. Common in Tysons homes with 100-amp service that was never upgraded. A load calculation tells us whether you need a new circuit, a new panel, or both.

My panel has Federal Pacific or Zinsco on the label

Both brands are documented fire risks. Federal Pacific Stab-Lok breakers fail to trip in measurable percentages of overcurrent events. Zinsco panels overheat at the bus bar. If you see either name, replacement is a safety call, not a maintenance call.

I still have a fuse box instead of breakers

Fuse panels haven’t been the standard since the 1960s. They’re not sized for modern loads — EV chargers, induction ranges, central AC — and replacement fuses are increasingly hard to find. A breaker panel upgrade brings the system to current code.

I want an EV charger but my panel is full

An EV charger needs a dedicated 40-50 amp circuit. If your panel has no empty slots or is only 100 amps, the charger installation starts with a panel upgrade. We size the new panel to handle the charger plus your existing load plus future additions.

Lights dim when the HVAC kicks on

A voltage sag when large appliances cycle usually points to an undersized panel, a loose neutral connection, or a Dominion Energy service drop that was sized for the old home on the lot. Diagnostic first — the panel upgrade follows if the cause traces to capacity.

The panel cover feels warm or smells like burning

Stop using the affected circuits and call us today. Heat at the panel almost always means a loose connection on a breaker or bus bar — the leading cause of residential electrical fires. We treat this as an urgent call and slot you in same-week.

Don’t see your issue?
Call 571-500-6637 — we’ll diagnose it.

Why Tysons homeowners call us for panel upgrade

Tysons, VA (formerly Tysons Corner) is Fairfax County’s urban center, located at the intersection of the Capital Beltway (I-495) and Route 123 (Chain Bridge Road). The Dulles Toll Road (Route 267) defines its northern edge. Tysons is bordered by McLean to the east, Vienna to the west, and Falls Church to the south. The Silver Line Metro (Tysons Corner and Spring Hill stations) connects the area to DC. Tysons is about 12 miles from downtown DC, 5 minutes from McLean and Vienna, and just 10–15 minutes from our Fairfax Blvd office.

1950s–1960s ramblers & early colonials

Pimmit Hills Border, Chain Bridge Road Corridor (original homes), Tysons Woods (early lots)

Before Tysons became an urban center, it was low-density farmland and scattered post-war subdivisions. The 1950s and 1960s homes that remain — mostly along the edges near Pimmit Hills and the Chain Bridge Road corridor — are modest ramblers and colonials with 100-amp panels and thermoplastic copper wiring. Many of these homes sit on lots now worth more than the structures, making them prime teardown candidates. Those still occupied often have decades of DIY modifications as owners squeezed more utility from aging infrastructure.

Symptoms: 100-amp panels with every slot filled and no room for modern additions. Ungrounded two-prong outlets in bedrooms and living areas. Decades of DIY wiring modifications — romex spliced to cloth-insulated wire, circuits extended without proper junction boxes. Undersized service entrance cables that were adequate for a 1,200-square-foot rambler but cannot support central AC and modern kitchen loads.

1970s–1990s colonials, split-levels & townhomes

Westpark, Tysons Woods, Spring Hill, Rotonda (condos), Chain Bridge Road Corridor (later construction)

Tysons’ residential growth peaked in the 1970s–1990s as the commercial center expanded and surrounding neighborhoods filled in. Westpark, Tysons Woods, and Spring Hill are dominated by colonials and split-levels with 150-amp or 200-amp panels and copper wiring adequate for their era. Some 1970s construction used aluminum branch wiring. These homes were built when Tysons was a suburban office park — now they sit in an urbanizing area with Metro access and rising property values, driving major renovation and addition activity that strains original electrical systems.

Symptoms: Aluminum branch wiring (1970s homes) with oxidized connections at outlets and switches. 150-amp panels that are full — no capacity for EV chargers, heat pump systems, or kitchen remodels. Federal Pacific Stab-Lok panels in some 1970s construction — a known fire hazard. Outdated sub-panels in finished basements that were added without load calculations.

2010s–2020s luxury new construction & teardown rebuilds

Scotts Run (new builds), International Drive Area, Spring Hill (infill), Pimmit Hills Border (rebuilds)

The Silver Line Metro’s arrival in 2014 accelerated Tysons’ transformation. Single-family teardown-rebuilds now produce 4,000–7,000-square-foot luxury homes on lots previously occupied by modest ramblers. New townhome communities and infill developments occupy gaps between commercial parcels. These homes are built to current code with 200-amp (sometimes 400-amp) panels, whole-house AFCI protection, and pre-wired EV charging circuits. But the rapid development pace means some builders cut corners on load calculations, and Dominion Energy service drops may not reflect the new home’s actual demand.

Symptoms: Nuisance AFCI breaker trips on motor-driven loads — vacuums, treadmills, and garage door openers. Undersized Dominion Energy service drops that were sized for the previous structure on the lot. Smart-home wiring conflicts — incorrectly shared neutrals on multi-wire branch circuits causing phantom loads. Voltage drop to detached garages and outdoor kitchens fed by undersized conductors run at build time.

Tysons is urbanizing fast — Silver Line Metro access, teardown rebuilds, and rising property values are driving major renovation activity. The panel is where every upgrade starts, and most Tysons homes have panels that were sized for a different era.

Specific situations we handle every week in Tysons

Here are the calls Ahmad gets most often from Tysons homeowners. If your situation matches one of these, you’re in the right place.

Frequent breaker trips

A breaker that trips once is doing its job. A breaker that trips daily, or that pops the moment you reset it, points to a panel that can’t handle the load you’re putting on it. We trace the circuit, measure the load, and tell you whether you need a new circuit, a new panel, or a fix upstream.

Fuse box instead of breakers

If your panel still has screw-in fuses instead of breakers, you’re on a system that hasn’t been the standard since the 1960s. Modern appliances draw loads fuse boxes weren’t designed for. We replace the fuse panel with a current-code 200-amp breaker panel that supports today’s electrical demand.

Federal Pacific or Zinsco panel

Both brands are documented fire risks — Federal Pacific Stab-Lok breakers fail to trip in measurable percentages of cases, and Zinsco panels overheat at the bus bar. If you have one, replacement is the safety call, not a maintenance call. We can identify the brand on-site in 5 minutes.

Lights dim when the AC or fridge kicks on

A voltage drop when a large appliance starts means your service can’t deliver consistent power. The cause is often an undersized panel, a loose neutral, or a feeder that wasn’t sized for what’s now drawing on it. Diagnostic first; upgrade if the cause traces to panel capacity.

Burning smell or warm panel cover

Stop using the affected circuits and call us today. Heat at the panel is almost always a loose connection on a breaker or bus bar, and loose connections in panels are the leading cause of electrical fires inside homes. We treat this as urgent.

Planning an EV charger or hot tub

Most older panels can’t safely take a continuous 40–50 amp load on top of the existing house demand. If you’re planning to add an EV charger, hot tub, or kitchen renovation, a panel upgrade often comes first. We size the upgrade to support both today’s load and what you’re adding.

Outdated 60- or 100-amp service

Homes built before 1965 often have 60-amp service; homes built 1965–2000 typically have 100-amp. Modern homes need 200-amp service to support HVAC, kitchen appliances, EV charging, and the rest of how you actually live. Upgrading is standard work, not exotic.

Adding a major addition or finished basement

A major remodel triggers a code-required load calculation. If the new load pushes past your panel’s safe capacity, the upgrade happens as part of the project. We coordinate the upgrade with the general contractor’s schedule so the inspector signs off the first time.

Our panel upgrade process — what happens when you call

When you call 571-500-6637 or request a quote online, here’s what happens.

1

A real conversation, not a script

We pick up the phone. You tell us what’s driving the upgrade — outdated panel, EV charger plans, home addition, frequent trips. We ask about your home’s age, your panel’s brand if you know it, and what’s on your wish list. If there’s any safety concern (burning smell, warm panel, sparking), we treat it as urgent and slot you in same-week.

2

Diagnostic visit and written estimate

We come to your house, open the panel, check the service entrance and meter, and run a load calculation against what you’re using today plus what you’re adding. You get a written estimate with the panel brand, amperage, breaker count, permit fee, and labor laid out clearly. No surprise pricing on the work day.

3

Permit and utility coordination

Most jurisdictions require a permit pulled by a licensed electrician for any panel upgrade. We file the permit, schedule the inspection, and coordinate with your utility for the temporary power-down. You don’t talk to the permit office or the utility — that’s our job.

4

The upgrade itself — typically one day

Morning: utility cuts power at the meter. We remove the old panel, install the new panel, re-land every circuit on the new breakers, and label them clearly. Afternoon: utility re-energizes the service, we power up, test every circuit, and walk you through the new panel. Most residential upgrades finish in one day.

5

Inspection and sign-off

The county inspector visits within a few days. We meet them at your house, walk them through the work, and they sign off. You get a copy of the permit and inspection record. The work is on the books with the county — protects your home insurance and your resale value.

How estimates work

Every panel upgrade starts with a diagnostic visit — we look at your panel, run a load calculation, and give you a written estimate before any work starts. No surprise charges on install day, no add-ons you didn’t agree to.

  • A diagnostic visit comes first. We look at the panel, the service entrance, and what’s drawing power. You get a written estimate before any work starts.
  • The estimate covers the panel hardware, the labor, the permit fee, and the utility coordination. No add-ons on the work day.
  • Major related work — service-entrance changes, meter-base replacements, sub-panels, EV-charger circuits — gets its own line item, not bundled in. You see what each piece costs.
  • After-hours and weekend work is available; we mention the premium up-front before booking.
No trip charge for Tysons, McLean, Vienna, Fairfax, or Falls Church. We don’t charge to drive to your house for the estimate.

We don’t post fixed prices because every house is different — service entrance condition, meter location, breaker count, code upgrades triggered by the work. The estimate after a real diagnostic visit is the only honest number.

About Ahmad Shaban, Master Electrician

Ahmad Shaban holds a Virginia Master Electrician license — the highest tier issued by the Commonwealth’s Department of Professional and Occupational Regulation. That license requires thousands of hours of supervised work and a state exam. EV Electric Services is fully insured, and the team is small by design: Ahmad and a trained apprentice on every job.

Ahmad is on every job site. He does the diagnostic visit, the load calculation, the panel swap, and the inspection walkthrough himself. No subcontractors, no project managers relaying your questions to someone else. When you call 571-500-6637, you’re talking to the person who opens the panel and does the work.

EV Electric Services holds a 5.0-star average across 148 customer reviews. Our review base is real, recent, and from Northern Virginia homeowners. We don’t ghost-write reviews or recycle them across business directories.

Tysons neighborhoods we serve

We cover all of Tysons, VA, including:

  • Westpark — 1970s–1980s single-family homes and townhomes on the McLean side of Tysons near Westpark Drive
  • Scotts Run — upscale 1980s–1990s colonials and new luxury builds near Scotts Run Stream Valley Park
  • Spring Hill — residential area near the Spring Hill Metro station with a mix of 1970s homes and new construction
  • Chain Bridge Road Corridor — established homes along Route 123 with lots dating to the 1960s–1970s amid rapid redevelopment
  • Pimmit Hills Border — 1950s ramblers at Tysons’ southeastern edge — many renovated or rebuilt as the area urbanizes
  • International Drive Area — newer luxury townhomes and single-family homes near Tysons’ corporate center and the Beltway
  • Tysons Woods — 1970s single-family homes on wooded lots tucked between Route 7 and the Dulles Toll Road
  • Rotonda — distinctive circular-plan condominium complex of 1970s high-rises near Tysons Corner Center

Outside Tysons, we serve Vienna, Fairfax, McLean, Falls Church, Arlington, and the rest of Fairfax County. We also cover Montgomery County, MD and Washington, DC.

Related electrical services in Tysons

A panel upgrade often happens alongside other electrical work — troubleshooting a persistent issue, adding an EV charger circuit, or upgrading smoke alarms while the panel is open. Here’s what else we do in Tysons.

Electrical Troubleshooting & Diagnostics
EV Charger Installation
Smoke Alarm Installation

Frequently asked questions

How much does an electrical panel upgrade cost in Tysons, VA?

Panel upgrade costs depend on your current panel’s condition, the new panel size, whether the service entrance or meter base needs replacement, and any code upgrades triggered by the work. A straightforward 100-to-200-amp swap with an existing service entrance in good condition is on the lower end. A job that requires a new meter base, service entrance cable, or Dominion Energy coordination costs more. We give you a written estimate after a diagnostic visit with every line item broken out — panel, labor, permit, utility coordination. No surprise pricing.

How long does a panel upgrade take?

Most residential panel upgrades finish in one day. Morning: Dominion Energy cuts power at the meter. We remove the old panel, install the new one, re-land every circuit on the new breakers, and label them clearly. Afternoon: the utility re-energizes the service, we power up, test every circuit, and walk you through the new panel. You’re back to full power before dinner. Jobs that involve service entrance replacement or a meter base swap may extend into a second day, but that’s uncommon.

Do I need a permit for a panel upgrade in Tysons, VA?

Yes — every panel upgrade in Fairfax County requires an electrical permit pulled by a licensed electrician. This is universal across Virginia jurisdictions, not optional. We file the permit application, schedule the post-install inspection with the county, and meet the inspector on-site. You don’t have to interact with the permit office or the utility. The permit and inspection record go on file with the county, which protects your home insurance and your resale value.

What are the signs I need to upgrade my electrical panel?

The most common signs: breakers that trip frequently under normal use, a panel that’s completely full with no room for new circuits, a warm or discolored panel cover, a burning smell near the panel, lights that dim when the HVAC cycles, or a panel with a Federal Pacific or Zinsco label (both are documented fire risks). If you’re planning to add an EV charger, hot tub, or major addition and your panel is 100 amps or less, the upgrade usually comes first. We can identify the issue in a 5-minute diagnostic visit.

What size panel do I need for my home?

200 amps is the modern standard for residential homes and has been since roughly 2015. Most homes built before 2000 have 100-amp or 150-amp service. If your home has central AC, an electric range, an EV charger, and standard modern appliances, 200 amps covers it. Homes with multiple EV chargers, electric heat, hot tubs, or large additions may need 320-amp or 400-amp service, though that’s less common. We run a load calculation on-site and recommend the right size based on what you have and what you’re adding.

Is my Federal Pacific or Zinsco panel dangerous?

Yes — both brands are documented safety hazards. Federal Pacific Stab-Lok breakers fail to trip on overcurrent in a measurable percentage of tested units. When a breaker doesn’t trip, the wire overheats, and that’s how electrical fires start. Zinsco panels have a different failure mode — the breakers fuse to the bus bar and overheat at the connection point. If you see either brand name on your panel, replacement is a safety call, not a someday project. We can identify the brand on-site in 5 minutes.

Can my panel handle an EV charger / hot tub / addition?

Often no — especially if your panel is 100 amps or has no empty breaker slots. An EV charger draws 40-50 amps continuous. A hot tub draws 40-60 amps. A major addition adds lighting, outlets, and HVAC circuits. Most older panels were sized for a fraction of today’s electrical demand. We run a load calculation that accounts for your current usage plus what you’re adding. If the panel can’t sustain the total, the upgrade happens first — then the new circuits go in on a panel with room to spare.

Does upgrading my panel increase my home’s value?

Yes. A modern 200-amp panel is a selling point — it tells buyers the home can handle EV chargers, modern kitchens, and smart-home systems without requiring an immediate electrical upgrade. Conversely, a Federal Pacific panel, a fuse box, or a 60-amp service is a red flag on any home inspection that often leads to price negotiations or repair credits. The upgrade also improves your home insurance standing — some insurers won’t cover homes with known-hazardous panels. The practical value is capacity and safety; the resale value follows from both.

Ready to Upgrade Your Panel?

Licensed electrical panel upgrades in Tysons, VA — proper load calculation, Dominion coordination, one-day install.
We respond within one business day.

content.rendered:
Home › Light Fixture Installation › This page

Light Fixture & Chandelier Installation in Fairfax, VA

Every Fixture Hung Right, Wired to Code

Ahmad Shaban is a Virginia-licensed Master Electrician who installs light fixtures and chandeliers across Fairfax every week. From a simple kitchen swap to a 200-pound foyer chandelier — proper mounting, clean wiring, tested and done.

Call 571-500-6637 Request a Quote

What light fixture installation involves

Light fixture installation covers everything from swapping a builder-grade flush mount for a pendant you actually chose, to hanging a heavy chandelier in a two-story foyer, to installing a full recessed lighting layout in a kitchen remodel. The service also includes outdoor wall sconces, bathroom vanity lights, under-cabinet lighting, and any fixture that connects to a junction box. A licensed electrician handles the wiring, the junction box mounting, the structural support for heavy fixtures, and the code compliance — work that matters for safety and for your home insurance.

Chandeliers are the specialty job. A heavy chandelier — 50 to 200-plus pounds — requires a fan-rated or weight-rated junction box, structural reinforcement in the ceiling, and safe rigging at height. Recessed lighting requires cutting ceiling holes, ensuring insulation clearance with IC-rated housings, and wiring multiple fixtures in series to a dimmer. If you bought a fixture online or at a showroom, we install it — any brand, any style, any weight. Just make sure it arrives with mounting hardware.

Ahmad Shaban is a Virginia-licensed Master Electrician and the owner of EV Electric Services, headquartered at 9488 Fairfax Blvd in Fairfax. Ahmad has installed chandeliers in McLean estates, recessed layouts in Vienna kitchens, and pendant lights in Fairfax townhomes. He handles the assessment, the install, and the dimmer setup personally. The team is small by design — Ahmad and a trained apprentice on every job.

Quick Reference

Common light fixture projects in Fairfax

Six fixture questions Fairfax homeowners ask before scheduling. Tap any for the answer.

Can you install a fixture I already bought?

Yes. We work with any brand, any style, any weight. Just make sure it arrives with mounting hardware and instructions. If parts are missing, we’ll tell you before we start so you can order them.

How heavy can a chandelier be?

Standard junction boxes support up to 50 lbs. Heavier chandeliers need a fan-rated box or a dedicated structural bracket bolted to a joist. We’ve hung fixtures over 200 lbs in two-story foyers — the ceiling support is the critical piece.

Do I need a dimmer with my new fixture?

Most LED fixtures work best with a compatible dimmer. If your existing switch is a standard toggle, we swap it for a dimmer as part of the install — no separate appointment needed. We match the dimmer to the fixture’s driver.

Can you add a light where there isn’t one now?

Yes. New locations require running wire from the nearest circuit, cutting a ceiling hole, and installing a junction box. Attic access makes it straightforward. Finished ceilings with no access above require fishing wire through the wall cavity.

How long does a fixture swap take?

A simple one-for-one replacement takes 30-60 minutes. Chandeliers and new-location installs take 2-3 hours. Recessed lighting layouts (6-8 fixtures) take half a day. We give you the time estimate before scheduling.

Do you install recessed lighting?

Yes. We install IC-rated recessed housings with proper insulation clearance, wire them in series, and connect to a dimmer. We lay out the fixtures to light the room evenly — not the builder-grade spacing that leaves dark corners.

Have a specific fixture in mind?
Call 571-500-6637 — we’ll tell you what’s involved.

Why Fairfax homeowners call us for light fixture installation

Fairfax is our home market. Our shop is at 9488 Fairfax Blvd, right on the Route 50/29 corridor. The City of Fairfax sits between Mantua to the north, Kings Park to the south, Fair Oaks to the west, and the Mosby Woods / Old Town border to the east — a tight mix of postwar ranches, 1960s-1970s colonials, 1990s-2000s townhomes, and pockets of new construction.

1950s-1970s split-levels & ranches

Mosby Woods, Country Club Hills, neighborhoods bordering Old Town

Built with 100-amp panels and circuits sized for the appliances of the era — toaster, fridge, color TV, a window unit if you were lucky. Fast-forward to 2026: induction range, central AC, two refrigerators, a tankless water heater, and an EV charger in the garage. Modern load patterns overwhelm circuits the original builder never planned for.

Symptoms: persistent breaker trips, warm outlets, dimming lights when large appliances start.

1960s-1980s colonials & traditionals

Mantua, Kings Park, older Greenbriar blocks

200-amp service but wired with the now-deprecated aluminum branch wiring for a window in the mid-1970s, or with GFCI gaps in kitchens and bathrooms that predate the 1996 code requirement.

Symptoms: outlets that worked yesterday and don’t today, intermittent power loss to one room, GFCIs that trip the moment a hairdryer plugs in.

1990s-2020s townhomes & newer detached

Fair Oaks, Fair Lakes, Fairfax Corner, Penderbrook

Newer homes are not exempt. AFCI breakers (the arc-fault interrupters required by modern code) are sensitive by design and trip on any noisy load. Smart-home wiring done by the previous owner’s “handy friend” can leave neutral wires bonded incorrectly.

Symptoms: failed dimmers, dead three-way switches, outlets that test fine but won’t hold a plug.

Fairfax homeowners are renovating kitchens, finishing basements, and updating builder-grade lighting across every era of housing. Whether it’s a 1960s colonial getting its first recessed layout or a 2010s townhome swapping flush mounts for pendants, the wiring has to be right.

Light fixture situations we handle every week in Fairfax

Here are the calls Ahmad gets most often from Fairfax homeowners. If your situation matches one of these, you’re in the right place.

Chandelier installation in a two-story foyer

Heavy chandeliers (50-200+ lbs) in two-story foyers require structural support rated for the fixture weight, proper junction box reinforcement, and safe access at height. We assess the ceiling structure, install a rated fan brace or mounting bracket, and wire the fixture to code. This is a two-person job — don’t attempt it with a YouTube video and a ladder.

Replacing an old fixture with a modern one

Swapping a flush-mount for a pendant, or a builder-grade fixture for something you actually chose, is the most common call we get. The wiring is usually in place but the junction box, dimmer switch, or mounting bracket may need updating. We handle the full swap — old fixture down, new fixture up, tested and working.

Installing a light where there wasn’t one before

Adding a new ceiling light means running wire from the nearest circuit, cutting a ceiling hole, installing a junction box, and connecting the fixture. If there’s attic access, it’s straightforward. If the ceiling is finished with no access above, we run conduit or fish wire through the wall cavity. Either way, it’s permitted work.

Recessed lighting installation

Recessed (can) lights require cutting holes in the ceiling, ensuring insulation clearance (IC-rated housings), running wire between each fixture, and connecting to a dimmer. We install 4, 6, 8, or more in a layout that actually lights the room evenly — not the builder-grade placement that leaves dark corners.

Outdoor lighting installation

Exterior wall sconces, soffit lights, pathway bollards, and landscape lighting all require weatherproof junction boxes, GFCI protection, and outdoor-rated wiring. We install exterior fixtures to code so they survive Northern Virginia weather and don’t trip your indoor circuits.

Ceiling fan to light fixture swap (or vice versa)

Ceiling fans and light fixtures use the same junction box location but different mounting hardware and wiring (fans need a fan-rated box for the vibration and weight). Swapping one for the other means verifying or upgrading the box, rewiring the switch (fan has two circuits — light and motor), and mounting the new unit.

Customer-supplied fixture — you bought it, we install it

You picked the perfect fixture online or at a showroom. We install it. We work with any brand, any style, any weight — just make sure it arrives with mounting hardware and instructions. If parts are missing, we’ll tell you before we start.

Our installation process — what happens when you call

When you call 571-500-6637 or request a quote online, here’s what happens.

1

Tell us about the fixture and the location

We ask what fixture you’re installing (or whether you need help choosing), where it’s going (room, ceiling type, height), and whether there’s existing wiring at the location. For chandeliers, we ask about weight and whether the ceiling is drywall-only or has structural backing.

2

On-site assessment if needed

For straightforward replacements, we can quote over the phone. For new locations, heavy chandeliers, or recessed lighting layouts, we visit the house, look at the ceiling, check the circuit, and give a written estimate. No charge for the visit in our primary service area.

3

Installation — typically 1-3 hours per fixture

We arrive with the tools and hardware. Simple replacements take 30-60 minutes. Chandeliers and new-location installs take 2-3 hours. Recessed lighting layouts (6-8 fixtures) take half a day. We test every fixture and clean up when we’re done.

4

Dimmer and switch upgrades included

If your new fixture needs a dimmer switch, or if the existing switch doesn’t match (toggle vs. rocker, single-pole vs. three-way), we swap it as part of the install. No separate appointment needed.

How estimates work

Light fixture pricing varies by job type — a simple swap costs less than a chandelier install or a new recessed layout. We give you a clear number before we start, and there are no add-ons on install day.

  • Simple fixture replacements (existing wiring, standard junction box) — per-fixture pricing given over the phone or on-site.
  • Chandelier installation — depends on weight, height, ceiling structure, and whether structural reinforcement is needed. Written estimate after on-site assessment.
  • New fixture locations (no existing wiring) — includes circuit run, junction box, switch, and fixture mount. Written estimate after on-site assessment.
  • Recessed lighting — priced per layout (number of fixtures + dimmer). Written estimate after measuring the room.
No trip charge for Fairfax, Vienna, McLean, Oakton, or Tysons.

About Ahmad Shaban, Master Electrician

Ahmad Shaban holds a Virginia Master Electrician license — the highest tier issued by the Commonwealth’s Department of Professional and Occupational Regulation. EV Electric Services is fully insured and headquartered at 9488 Fairfax Blvd in Fairfax. The team is small by design: Ahmad and a trained apprentice on every job.

Chandeliers are Ahmad’s specialty. He’s hung fixtures in two-story McLean foyers, installed recessed layouts in Vienna great rooms, and swapped out builder-grade lighting in Fairfax townhomes. Heavy fixtures, high ceilings, and tricky ceiling structures are routine — not special occasions. Ahmad assesses the ceiling, installs the structural support, and wires the fixture himself.

EV Electric Services holds a 5.0-star average across 148 customer reviews. Our review base is real, recent, and from Northern Virginia homeowners. We don’t ghost-write reviews or recycle them across business directories.

Fairfax neighborhoods we serve

We cover all of Fairfax, VA, including:

  • Old Town Fairfax — the original City of Fairfax core around Main Street and Chain Bridge Road
  • Mantua — the established mid-century neighborhood north of Pickett Road
  • Kings Park / Kings Park West — 1960s-1970s colonials south of Braddock Road
  • Mosby Woods — split-levels and ranches near the City of Fairfax border
  • Country Club Hills — mid-century homes near the Country Club of Fairfax
  • Fair Oaks / Fair Lakes — newer townhomes and detached homes along the Route 50 / 66 corridor
  • Fairfax Corner — 2000s townhomes and condos at the I-66 / West Ox interchange
  • Penderbrook — 1980s-1990s townhomes around the Penderbrook golf course
  • Greenbriar — 1970s-1980s neighborhood east of Route 50, near Fair Oaks Mall

Outside Fairfax City, we serve Vienna, McLean, Oakton, Tysons, Burke, Annandale, Falls Church, and the rest of Fairfax County. We also cover DC and Montgomery County, MD (Rockville, Bethesda, Chevy Chase, Potomac).

Related electrical services in Fairfax

Light fixture work often connects to other electrical needs — a panel that needs a new circuit for recessed lighting, outlets that should be upgraded while we’re in the walls, or troubleshooting a dimmer that flickers. Here’s what else we do in Fairfax.

Electrical Troubleshooting
Outlet & Switch Installation
Electrical Panel Upgrades
EV Charger Installation
Smoke Alarm Installation

Frequently asked questions

How much does it cost to install a light fixture in Fairfax, VA?

It depends on the fixture type, whether there’s existing wiring, and the complexity of the mounting. A simple one-for-one replacement — same location, existing junction box, no structural changes — is on the lower end. A chandelier that requires structural reinforcement, a new fixture location that needs a circuit run, or a recessed lighting layout with 6-8 fixtures costs more. We quote simple swaps over the phone. For chandeliers, new locations, and recessed layouts, we give a written estimate after an on-site look. No surprises on install day.

Can I install a chandelier myself?

For a lightweight fixture under 25 pounds on an existing junction box with existing wiring, a handy homeowner can manage it. But heavy chandeliers — 50 pounds and up — are a different job entirely. They require a fan-rated or weight-rated junction box, structural reinforcement bolted to a ceiling joist, safe rigging at height, and proper wire connections. A 150-pound chandelier falling from a two-story foyer ceiling is not a risk worth taking. We handle the structural assessment, the box upgrade, and the wiring so the fixture stays where you put it.

How long does it take to install a light fixture?

A simple fixture swap — existing wiring, standard junction box — takes 30-60 minutes. Chandelier installations and new fixture locations (where we need to run wire and cut a ceiling opening) take 2-3 hours. A full recessed lighting layout with 6-8 fixtures and a dimmer takes half a day. We give you the time estimate before scheduling so you can plan around it. Most jobs are same-day — you have working lights before we leave.

Can you install a light where there isn’t one now?

Yes. Adding a new ceiling light means running wire from the nearest circuit, cutting a ceiling hole, installing a code-compliant junction box, and connecting the fixture. If there’s attic access above the location, the job is straightforward — we run the wire through the attic and drop it down. If the ceiling is finished with no attic access, we fish wire through the wall cavity or run surface-mounted conduit. Either way, it gets permitted and inspected.

Do I need a permit for light fixture installation?

For a simple fixture swap on existing wiring — taking down an old fixture and putting up a new one — no permit is typically required. For new fixture locations that involve running a new circuit, cutting into walls or ceilings, or adding a junction box, Fairfax County usually requires an electrical permit. The rule of thumb: if we’re adding new wiring, it gets permitted. We handle the permit process and schedule the inspection.

Can you install a fixture I bought myself?

Yes — we install customer-supplied fixtures every week. We work with any brand, any style, any weight. Just make sure it arrives with the mounting hardware and instructions. If parts are missing or the manufacturer packed the wrong bracket, we’ll tell you before we start so you can get replacements. We don’t mark up fixtures — you buy what you want, we install it.

What’s the difference between a regular junction box and a fan-rated box?

A standard junction box is designed for lightweight fixtures — typically up to 50 pounds. A fan-rated (or weight-rated) box is reinforced and mounted to the ceiling structure to support heavier loads and the vibration that comes with ceiling fans. If your chandelier weighs more than 50 pounds, or if you’re installing a ceiling fan, code requires a fan-rated box. We assess the existing box during the install and upgrade it if needed — it’s a common part of chandelier work.

Do you install recessed lighting?

Yes. We install IC-rated recessed housings (insulation-contact rated, required when insulation is present above the ceiling), wire them in series, and connect the layout to a compatible dimmer. We plan the fixture spacing to light the room evenly — not the builder-grade placement that clusters lights in the center and leaves the edges dark. A typical kitchen or living room layout with 6-8 fixtures takes about half a day.

Ready to Get That Fixture Installed?

Light fixture and chandelier installation in Fairfax, VA — proper mounting, clean wiring, same-day service.
We respond within one business day.

title.rendered: Smoke Alarm Installation in Fairfax, VA

content.rendered:
Home › Smoke Alarm Installation › This page

Smoke Alarm Installation in Fairfax, VA

Hardwired, Interconnected, Code-Compliant

Ahmad Shaban is a Virginia-licensed Master Electrician who installs and upgrades smoke alarm systems across Fairfax. Hardwired interconnected alarms, CO detectors, code-compliance assessments — done right, documented, and inspectable.

Call 571-500-6637 Request a Quote

What smoke alarm installation involves

Smoke alarm installation means wiring your home’s fire detection system so it actually works when it matters. Battery-only alarms are standalone units — each one operates independently, and when one triggers, the others stay silent. Hardwired interconnected alarms connect to a dedicated circuit and communicate with each other, so when one alarm detects smoke in the basement, every alarm in the house sounds. A licensed electrician handles the junction box wiring, circuit integration, and code-compliant placement that makes interconnected systems work reliably.

Virginia fire code requires smoke alarms in every bedroom, outside each sleeping area, and on every level of the home. All smoke alarms must be replaced every 10 years — check the manufacture date on the back of each unit. When you sell a home or complete a major renovation, you may be required to upgrade to hardwired interconnected alarms. In new construction, interconnected systems have been code-required for years. If your home predates that requirement, upgrading is the single best fire-safety improvement you can make.

Ahmad Shaban is a Virginia-licensed Master Electrician and the owner of EV Electric Services, headquartered at 9488 Fairfax Blvd — right here in Fairfax. Smoke alarm work is straightforward but detail-dependent: correct placement, proper interconnection, CO detector integration, and documentation that holds up at inspection. Ahmad handles every install personally and treats fire-safety work as priority scheduling.

Quick Reference

Common smoke alarm projects in Fairfax

Six smoke alarm questions Fairfax homeowners ask most. Tap any for the answer.

How do I know if my smoke alarms are expired?

Flip the alarm over and look for the manufacture date stamped on the back. If it’s more than 10 years old, it’s expired regardless of whether it still chirps when you press the test button. Yellowed plastic is another sign — UV degradation tracks closely with sensor degradation.

Battery-only or hardwired — which should I get?

Hardwired interconnected alarms are the gold standard. They connect to a household circuit (with battery backup) and communicate — when one triggers, all sound. Battery-only alarms work independently. For a home you’re living in long-term, hardwired is worth the investment.

Do I need CO detectors too?

Virginia code requires carbon monoxide detectors on every level of a home with a fuel-burning appliance (gas furnace, water heater, stove) or an attached garage. Combination smoke/CO units cover both requirements in one device.

My alarm goes off every time I cook — what gives?

The alarm is either too close to the kitchen (code says 10 feet minimum from cooking appliances) or past its 10-year life and oversensitive. Modern units have better nuisance-alarm resistance. We relocate and replace.

I’m selling my house — do I need to upgrade?

The buyer’s inspector will flag missing, expired, or non-interconnected alarms. Getting compliant before listing avoids last-minute inspection delays and repair credits. We assess the house and provide documentation for your listing agent.

How long does a full-house install take?

A standard 3-4 bedroom home takes 2-4 hours for hardwired interconnected smoke and CO alarms. We run the wiring, mount each unit, test every alarm individually and as a system, and label manufacture dates for the 10-year replacement cycle.

Not sure what you need?
Call 571-500-6637 — we’ll assess your home.

Why Fairfax homeowners call us for smoke alarm installation

Fairfax is our home market. Our shop is at 9488 Fairfax Blvd, right on the Route 50/29 corridor. The City of Fairfax sits between Mantua to the north, Kings Park to the south, Fair Oaks to the west, and the Mosby Woods / Old Town border to the east — a tight mix of postwar ranches, 1960s-1970s colonials, 1990s-2000s townhomes, and pockets of new construction.

1950s-1970s split-levels & ranches

Mosby Woods, Country Club Hills, neighborhoods bordering Old Town

Built with 100-amp panels and circuits sized for the appliances of the era — toaster, fridge, color TV, a window unit if you were lucky. Fast-forward to 2026: induction range, central AC, two refrigerators, a tankless water heater, and an EV charger in the garage. Modern load patterns overwhelm circuits the original builder never planned for.

Symptoms: persistent breaker trips, warm outlets, dimming lights when large appliances start.

1960s-1980s colonials & traditionals

Mantua, Kings Park, older Greenbriar blocks

200-amp service but wired with the now-deprecated aluminum branch wiring for a window in the mid-1970s, or with GFCI gaps in kitchens and bathrooms that predate the 1996 code requirement.

Symptoms: outlets that worked yesterday and don’t today, intermittent power loss to one room, GFCIs that trip the moment a hairdryer plugs in.

1990s-2020s townhomes & newer detached

Fair Oaks, Fair Lakes, Fairfax Corner, Penderbrook

Newer homes are not exempt. AFCI breakers (the arc-fault interrupters required by modern code) are sensitive by design and trip on any noisy load. Smart-home wiring done by the previous owner’s “handy friend” can leave neutral wires bonded incorrectly.

Symptoms: failed dimmers, dead three-way switches, outlets that test fine but won’t hold a plug.

Fairfax’s housing stock spans seven decades — from 1950s ranches with no hardwired alarms to 2020s townhomes with AFCI-protected circuits. Whatever era your home falls in, the fire code applies the same way, and the alarms need to work together.

Smoke alarm situations we handle every week in Fairfax

Here are the calls Ahmad gets most often from Fairfax homeowners. If your situation matches one of these, you’re in the right place.

Battery-only smoke alarms chirping or expired

Battery-only alarms expire after 10 years (sealed-battery models) or need battery replacement annually. If yours are chirping, yellowed, or you can’t remember when they were installed, they’re due. We can replace them with hardwired interconnected units so all alarms sound together — the code-required standard for new construction and renovations.

Upgrading to hardwired interconnected alarms

Older homes often have standalone battery-only alarms that don’t communicate with each other. If a downstairs alarm triggers, the upstairs bedrooms hear nothing. Hardwired interconnected alarms wire together on a single circuit — when one triggers, all sound throughout the house. We run the wiring, install the alarms, and test every unit.

Smoke alarm code compliance for a home sale

When you sell a home in Virginia, the buyer’s inspector will flag non-compliant smoke alarms — missing from bedrooms, expired units, or non-interconnected systems. Getting compliant before listing avoids last-minute inspection delays. We assess the house, install what’s needed, and give you documentation for the listing agent.

Adding carbon monoxide detectors

Virginia code requires CO detectors on every level of a dwelling with a fuel-burning appliance or an attached garage. Many homes have smoke alarms but no CO detectors, or have standalone CO detectors that aren’t interconnected with the smoke alarms. We install combination smoke/CO units that cover both requirements in one device.

Post-renovation smoke alarm placement

A finished basement, a new bedroom, or a major remodel triggers code requirements for smoke alarms in the new space. We add hardwired alarms in the new areas and integrate them with the existing system so the whole house is connected.

False alarms from old or poorly placed detectors

Smoke alarms that trigger every time you cook, run the shower, or open the oven are usually either too close to the kitchen/bathroom or past their 10-year life. We relocate and replace with modern units that have better nuisance-alarm resistance while still meeting code placement requirements.

Our installation process — what happens when you call

When you call 571-500-6637 or request a quote online, here’s what happens.

1

We assess your current setup

We ask about your home — how many levels, how many bedrooms, whether you have hardwired or battery-only alarms, and whether you have gas appliances or an attached garage (triggers CO detector requirements). For phone quotes on straightforward replacements, this is enough. For upgrades to hardwired interconnected, we visit.

2

On-site assessment for hardwired upgrades

We walk the house, count alarm locations, check attic/crawl access for running wire, and assess the circuit. You get a written estimate covering the alarm units, the wiring, and the labor. No surprise pricing.

3

Installation — typically 2-4 hours for a full house

For a standard 3-4 bedroom home, hardwired interconnected smoke/CO installation takes 2-4 hours. We run the wiring, mount each alarm, connect them, and test every unit — individually and as a system (triggering one to verify all sound). We label each alarm’s manufacture date for the 10-year replacement cycle.

4

Testing and documentation

We test every alarm, verify interconnection, and provide a written record of what was installed, where, and when. This documentation is useful for home insurance, for the next buyer’s inspection, and for the 10-year replacement schedule.

How estimates work

Smoke alarm pricing depends on the scope — battery-only swaps are simple, hardwired interconnected upgrades involve wiring. Here’s how we break it down so you know what you’re paying for.

  • Battery-only replacement (no wiring change). Per-alarm pricing, quoted over the phone. We carry Kidde, First Alert, and BRK units on the truck — most replacements finish in one visit.
  • Hardwired interconnected upgrade (full house). Depends on the number of alarms, wiring access, and home layout. A standard 3-4 bedroom home takes 2-4 hours. Written estimate after on-site assessment.
  • Single-room additions (post-renovation). Per-alarm pricing plus the wiring run to tie into the existing circuit. Quoted on-site after we see the access path.
  • Code-compliance assessment. We walk the house, identify what’s needed for Virginia fire code compliance, and provide a written quote. No charge for the assessment in our primary service area.
No trip charge for Fairfax, Vienna, McLean, Oakton, or Tysons.

About Ahmad Shaban, Master Electrician

Ahmad Shaban holds a Virginia Master Electrician license — the highest tier issued by the Commonwealth’s Department of Professional and Occupational Regulation. EV Electric Services is fully insured and headquartered at 9488 Fairfax Blvd, right here in the City of Fairfax. The team is small by design: Ahmad and a trained apprentice on every job.

Smoke alarm work is code-compliance work. Ahmad knows Virginia fire code placement requirements, interconnection standards, and the CO detector mandates that apply to homes with gas appliances or attached garages. Every install gets tested — each alarm individually, and the full system as a group — with documentation you can hand to an inspector or a buyer’s agent.

EV Electric Services holds a 5.0-star average across 148 customer reviews. Our review base is real, recent, and from Northern Virginia homeowners. We don’t ghost-write reviews or recycle them across business directories.

Fairfax neighborhoods we serve

We cover all of Fairfax, VA, including:

  • Old Town Fairfax — the original City of Fairfax core around Main Street and Chain Bridge Road
  • Mantua — the established mid-century neighborhood north of Pickett Road
  • Kings Park / Kings Park West — 1960s-1970s colonials south of Braddock Road
  • Mosby Woods — split-levels and ranches near the City of Fairfax border
  • Country Club Hills — mid-century homes near the Country Club of Fairfax
  • Fair Oaks / Fair Lakes — newer townhomes and detached homes along the Route 50 / 66 corridor
  • Fairfax Corner — 2000s townhomes and condos at the I-66 / West Ox interchange
  • Penderbrook — 1980s-1990s townhomes around the Penderbrook golf course
  • Greenbriar — 1970s-1980s neighborhood east of Route 50, near Fair Oaks Mall

Outside Fairfax City, we serve Vienna, McLean, Oakton, Tysons, Burke, Annandale, Falls Church, and the rest of Fairfax County. We also cover DC and Montgomery County, MD (Rockville, Bethesda, Chevy Chase, Potomac).

Related electrical services in Fairfax

Smoke alarm work often happens alongside other electrical projects — a panel upgrade that frees up a circuit for interconnected alarms, or a troubleshooting visit that reveals expired detectors. Here’s what else we do in Fairfax.

Electrical Troubleshooting
Electrical Panel Upgrades
Outlet & Switch Installation
Light Fixtures & Chandeliers
EV Charger Installation

Frequently asked questions

How much does smoke alarm installation cost in Fairfax, VA?

Pricing depends on the scope of work. Battery-only replacements are per-alarm and can be quoted over the phone — we carry Kidde, First Alert, and BRK units on the truck. Hardwired interconnected upgrades depend on the number of alarms, your home’s wiring access (attic, crawl space, finished ceilings), and the layout. A standard 3-4 bedroom home typically takes 2-4 hours. We provide a written estimate after an on-site assessment so you see the full cost before we start. No trip charge in our primary service area.

How often should smoke alarms be replaced?

Every 10 years, per both the manufacturer and NFPA (National Fire Protection Association) guidelines. The manufacture date is stamped on the back of the unit — not the date you installed it, but the date it was made. After 10 years, the sensors degrade regardless of whether the alarm still chirps when you press the test button. If your alarms are yellowed, you can’t read the date, or you simply don’t remember when they went in, it’s time. We check every unit during an assessment.

What’s the difference between hardwired and battery-only smoke alarms?

Battery-only alarms run on a battery and operate independently — each one is on its own. If a downstairs alarm triggers, the upstairs bedrooms hear nothing. Hardwired alarms connect to a dedicated household circuit and have a battery backup for power outages. When they’re interconnected, triggering one alarm sounds all alarms throughout the house simultaneously. That interconnection is what saves lives in the middle of the night when a fire starts two floors away from your bedroom.

Does Virginia require interconnected smoke alarms?

Yes — for new construction and major renovations, Virginia code requires hardwired interconnected smoke alarms. Existing homes may be grandfathered under the code that was in effect when they were built, but that doesn’t mean standalone battery alarms are adequate for your family’s safety. If you’re selling the home, the buyer’s inspector will flag non-interconnected alarms. We recommend upgrading regardless of whether code technically requires it — the cost is modest and the safety improvement is significant.

Do I need carbon monoxide detectors too?

If your home has a fuel-burning appliance — gas furnace, gas water heater, gas stove, fireplace — or an attached garage, Virginia code requires carbon monoxide detectors on every level of the dwelling. Many homes have smoke alarms but no CO detectors, or have standalone CO detectors that aren’t interconnected with the smoke alarm system. We install combination smoke/CO units that cover both requirements in one device and tie into the interconnected system.

My smoke alarm keeps going off when I cook — what should I do?

Two likely causes. First, the alarm may be too close to the kitchen — code requires a minimum of 10 feet from cooking appliances, and many older installs violate this. Second, the alarm may be past its 10-year life and oversensitive as the sensor degrades. Modern photoelectric alarms have significantly better nuisance-alarm resistance than older ionization models. We relocate the alarm to a code-compliant position and replace it with a current-generation unit that won’t cry wolf every time you use the oven.

Do I need a permit for smoke alarm installation?

For straightforward battery-only replacements, no permit is needed. For hardwired interconnected systems that involve running new wiring or adding a dedicated circuit, Fairfax County typically requires an electrical permit. The permit ensures the work is inspected and documented — important for home insurance and resale. We pull the permit and handle the inspection if one is required. Most simple hardwired replacements on existing wiring don’t trigger a permit, but new circuit work does.

Can you install smoke alarms for a home sale inspection?

Yes — this is one of the most common calls we get. We assess the house for Virginia fire code compliance, identify what’s missing or expired, install the needed alarms, and provide written documentation for your listing agent. Getting compliant before listing avoids last-minute inspection surprises and repair-credit negotiations. We can usually complete a full-house assessment and install in a single visit, and we prioritize scheduling for sellers with closing deadlines.

Get Your Smoke Alarms Right

Hardwired interconnected smoke alarm installation in Fairfax, VA — code-compliant, tested, documented.
We respond within one business day.
